chunk_summary: Guidance on addressing texture delay in complementary feeding, including troubleshooting, red flags for referral, and staged progression strategies for children aged 6–24 months.
learning_objectives: Identify red flags for swallowing dysfunction and aspiration in children with texture delay.
Understand the staged approach to texture progression for puree-dependent children.
Learn caregiver coaching strategies to improve feeding outcomes and prevent feeding aversion.
Recognize when to refer for feeding evaluation and screen for nutritional deficiencies.
key_facts: Mild gagging during texture introduction is normal and improves with calm, repeated exposure.
Red flags for aspiration include coughing, choking, wet voice, recurrent chest infections, prolonged feeding times (>30 minutes), and poor weight gain.
Texture delay persisting beyond 10 months despite structured coaching warrants referral for feeding evaluation.
Staged progression includes thickened purees, soft lumps, fork-mashed textures, and finely chopped family foods.
Children with neurodevelopmental delay, CP, or craniofacial anomalies are at higher risk of feeding difficulties.
Screen for anemia and micronutrient gaps in children with limited textures or milk displacement.
reference_quote: Escalate concern when there are signs of swallowing dysfunction or aspiration: coughing or choking with feeds, wet or gurgly voice after swallowing, recurrent chest infections, prolonged feeding times (>30 minutes), fatigue during meals, or poor weight gain despite caregiver effort. (ASHA practice portal)
document_key_topics: Nutritional screening in children with feeding difficulties
